test_cases.question: What were the main types of mistakes noticed in assessment of Co-operative Societies and Co-operative Banks?

expected.answer_summary: The main types of mistakes noticed were: (1) Mistakes in levy of interest/penalty - 277 cases with Rs. 40.49 crore tax effect; (2) Irregular allowance of expenditure, deductions - 184 cases with Rs. 376.07 crore; (3) Mistakes in computation of income, tax, surcharge - 104 cases with Rs. 1,315.93 crore; (4) Income not assessed/under assessed - 43 cases with Rs. 22.24 crore; (5) Mistakes related to TDS provisions - 38 cases with Rs. 45.63 crore; (6) Irregular set-off of loss - 36 cases with Rs. 147.89 crore; (7) Incorrect allowance of depreciation - 22 cases with Rs. 54.64 crore; (8) Other mistakes - 12 cases with Rs. 1.12 crore; (9) Overassessment of income/Overcharge - 11 cases with Rs. 577.95 crore; (10) Unexplained investment/expenditure - 4 cases with Rs. 9,616.23 crore.
key_facts: 277 cases of mistakes in levy of interest/penalty
184 cases of irregular allowance of expenditure and deductions
104 cases of mistakes in computation of income, tax, surcharge
Unexplained investment/expenditure had highest tax effect of Rs. 9,616.23 crore despite only 4 cases

must_not_contain: section 80P deductions which is covered in Chapter 3

Total 649 cases with Rs. 694.50 crore tax effect.
key_facts: Section 80P(2)(d) had highest number of irregularities - 367 cases
Section 36(1)(viia) had highest tax effect - Rs. 375.20 crore
Section 80P(2)(a)(i) had 115 cases - second highest count

test_cases.question: What were the objectives of the performance audit on Assessment of Co-operative Societies and Co-operative Banks?

expected.answer_summary: The objectives of the performance audit were to examine: (i) whether all entities in the Co-operative Sector are in the tax net and filing income tax returns and are being assessed for levy of due amount of tax; (ii) the nature and extent of compliance to provisions specific to the assessees of Co-operative Sector under the Income Tax Act, 1961; and (iii) the nature and extent of compliance to the general provisions of the Act during assessment process.
key_facts: Objective to examine coverage of Co-operative Societies in tax net
Objective to examine compliance to specific provisions under IT Act 1961
Objective to examine compliance to general provisions during assessment

test_cases.question: Why were Co-operative Banks denied deduction under section 80P of the Act from assessment year 2007-08?

expected.answer_summary: From assessment year 2007-08 onwards, deduction under Section 80P of the Act is not available to any Co-operative Bank as per section 80P(4) of the Act. However, a Primary Agricultural Credit Society (PACS) or a Primary Co-operative Agricultural and Rural Development Bank will continue to claim the benefit of deduction under Section 80P of the Act. CBDT also clarified through Circular No. 6/2010 dated September 20, 2010 that Regional Rural Banks are not eligible for deduction under Section 80P.
key_facts: Section 80P(4) denies deduction to Co-operative Banks from AY 2007-08
PACS and Primary Co-operative Agricultural and Rural Development Banks are still eligible
CBDT Circular No. 6/2010 clarified Regional Rural Banks are ineligible

test_cases.question: What was the total number of surveys conducted on Co-operative Societies and Banks during 2014-15 to 2018-19 and how many new assessees were identified?

expected.answer_summary: During 2014-15 to 2018-19, out of 806 total surveys conducted, only 39 surveys were conducted on Co-operative Societies and 33 on Co-operative Banks. No new assessees were identified in Co-operative Societies through surveys. Only 2 new assessees of Co-operative Banks were identified (in 2016-17) and they filed returns. The mechanism of surveys was not effectively utilized by ITD.
key_facts: 806 total surveys were conducted during the period
39 surveys on Co-operative Societies, 33 on Co-operative Banks
No new Co-operative Society assessees identified
Only 2 new Co-operative Bank assessees identified in 2016-17

test_cases.question: What are the main audit recommendations regarding PAN registration and information sharing for Co-operative Societies?

expected.answer_summary: Key audit recommendations included: (1) CBDT may request Central and State level registering bodies for instituting seeding of PAN in their databases and facilitate structured institutional sharing of information; (2) CBDT may ensure ITD checks actual status of applicant vis-a-vis name and activity while allotting PAN; (3) ITD may consider affixing fourth letter as 'A' to PAN of Co-operative Society for easy identification and monitoring; (4) Evidential proof of certificate of registration by Registrar and details of members is essential for completing assessments; (5) ITD may issue instructions to ensure accounts are audited by empanelled auditors only.
key_facts: Recommendation for PAN seeding in databases of registering bodies
Recommendation for structured information sharing
Recommendation for fourth letter of PAN to be 'A' for Co-operative Societies
Recommendation for verification of registration certificates
Recommendation for audit by empanelled auditors

test_cases.question: What were the findings regarding unexplained cash credits and unexplained investments in Co-operative Societies?

expected.answer_summary: Audit noticed 4 cases in 3 states (Madhya Pradesh, Maharashtra, and Uttar Pradesh) where ITD had not taken into account unexplained cash credit and unexplained investment while completing assessment, resulting in short levy of tax of Rs. 9,616.23 crore. In one notable case at PCIT-1 Lucknow, the AO applied only 25 per cent on deposits of Rs. 17,877.54 crore and shareholders fund of Rs. 327.75 crore, whereas the entire amount should have been treated as unexplained cash credit under section 68 of the Act, resulting in short charge of tax of Rs. 5,737.94 crore.
key_facts: 4 cases in 3 states with unexplained cash credits/investments
Tax effect of Rs. 9,616.23 crore
Major case involved deposits of Rs. 17,877.54 crore treated inadequately
Section 68 of Act applies to unexplained cash credits

key_amounts.context: Tax effect of unexplained investment/expenditure cases
Deposits not properly treated as unexplained cash credit in one case
Short charge of tax in Lucknow case

test_cases.question: What was the total deduction claimed and allowed under section 80P(2)(d) of the Act and what were the reasons for disallowance?

expected.answer_summary: In 553 cases across 14 states/regions, deduction of Rs. 455.63 crore was allowed based on claims made on income of Rs. 655.48 crore under section 80P(2)(d) of the Act. Of these, in 126 cases the AOs disallowed entire claim of Rs. 150.31 crore, in 347 cases entire claim of Rs. 366.15 crore was allowed, and in 79 cases partial allowance of Rs. 86.93 crore was made against claims of Rs. 136.47 crore. Reasons for disallowance included: 80P(2)(d) not applicable to Co-operative Banks other than PACS, interest from surplus funds in Co-operative Societies not eligible, interest from Co-operative Banks not shown in P&L account.
key_facts: 553 cases examined for section 80P(2)(d) claims
Rs. 455.63 crore deduction was allowed out of Rs. 655.48 crore claimed
126 cases had entire claim of Rs. 150.31 crore disallowed
Section applies to interest and dividend from investments in other Co-operative Societies

key_amounts.context: Total income on which claim was made

test_cases.question: What was the period covered by this performance audit and how many assessment cases were actually covered?

expected.answer_summary: The performance audit covered assessments of Co-operative Societies and Co-operative Banks completed during financial years 2014-15 to 2018-19. Out of 9,282 cases called for, 400 cases did not fall under the category of Co-operative Societies and Banks, and 412 cases were not produced. Therefore, 8,470 cases (95.36 per cent of remaining 8,882 cases) were covered in the performance audit.
key_facts: Period covered: FY 2014-15 to 2018-19
8,470 cases were actually covered
400 cases did not fall under Co-operative Societies/Banks category
412 cases (4.44 per cent) were not produced
95.36 per cent of valid cases were produced

test_cases.question: What were the findings on differential treatment by Assessing Officers for similar Co-operative entities?

expected.answer_summary: Audit found that Assessing Officers adopted differential approaches in assessments: (1) For section 80P(2)(d), in Karnataka 49 cases treated interest from Co-operative Banks as 'income from other sources' while 50 cases treated them as business income; (2) In Maharashtra, 6 cases (3 assessees) had differential treatment - interest from Co-operative Banks allowed in one AY but denied in another; (3) For Regional Rural Banks, Land Development Banks and Agriculture Rural Development Banks, deduction under section 80P was allowed in 106 cases (Rs. 751.5 crore) but entirely disallowed in 50 cases (Rs. 2,035.3 crore claim). This indicated lack of uniformity in applying the same law.
key_facts: Assessing Officers taking differential stands on similar cases
Interest from Co-operative Banks treated differently across assessees
106 cases allowed deduction for rural/agriculture banks, 50 cases denied entirely
Recommendation to examine reasons for variations and issue directions for consistency

key_amounts.context: Cases where section 80P deduction was allowed for rural banks
Cases where entire section 80P claim was disallowed

test_cases.question: In how many cases was the registration certificate not available in assessment folders and what was the audit finding?

expected.answer_summary: Out of 8,470 assessment cases examined in audit, in 4,376 cases the registration certificate was not available in the assessment folders. This indicated that the system of keeping evidential proof of registration status in ITD is not foolproof. In absence of registration certificate, audit could not ascertain how Assessing Officers ensured genuineness of claims of deduction under section 80P. Additionally, in 842 cases status of registration was not examined, and in 5,343 cases it could not be ascertained whether verification was done.
key_facts: 4,376 cases had no registration certificate in assessment folders
842 cases where registration status was not examined
5,343 cases where verification status could not be ascertained
System for evidential proof not foolproof

key_amounts.context: Cases where registration status not examined
Cases where verification status could not be ascertained

test_cases.question: What amendments were made to the Income Tax Act based on Audit recommendations?

expected.answer_summary: Based on Performance Audit Reports, several amendments were made: (1) From Report No. 27 of 2017 on Medical Sector - Rule 18AB notified, new provisions in Sections 80G(2)(vii), 80G(2)(ix) and Section 35(1A) via Finance Act 2020, and provisos to Section 11 via Finance Act 2020; (2) From Report No. 1 of 2019 on Entertainment Sector - Finance Act 2020 modified definition of Royalty in Section 9 to include cinematographic films; (3) From Report No. 14 of 2020 on Search and Seizure - Finance Act 2022 introduced new Section 79A and Finance Act 2021 amended Sections 153A/153C.
key_facts: Rule 18AB notified vide Notification No 19 of 2021
New provisions in Section 80G via Finance Act 2020
Section 9 definition of Royalty modified
New Section 79A introduced via Finance Act 2022
Sections 153A/153C amended via Finance Act 2021

test_cases.question: What were the persistent and pervasive irregularities noticed in Corporation Tax and Income Tax assessments?

expected.answer_summary: Audit identified persistent and pervasive irregularities including: (i) Irregularities in allowing depreciation/business losses/capital losses; (ii) Incorrect allowance of business expenditure; (iii) Excess or irregular refunds/interest on refunds; (iv) Mistakes under special provisions including MAT/AMT/Tonnage Tax. These irregularities occurred despite ITBA implementation, indicating lack of appropriate controls and effective monitoring. For Corporation Tax, Delhi had 79 observations with tax effect of ₹5,041.71 crore out of total ₹7,788.98 crore. For Income Tax, Delhi had 31 observations while Maharashtra had highest tax effect of ₹109.75 crore.

test_cases.question: How many times has the FRBM Act been amended since its enactment and what are the key fiscal targets?

expected.answer_summary: The FRBM Act was enacted on 26 August 2003 and has been amended four times - in 2004, 2012, 2015, and 2018. The key targets set by the 2018 Amendment Act (to be achieved by end of FY 2024-25) are: General Government debt not to exceed 60 per cent of GDP, Central Government debt not to exceed 40 per cent of GDP, and additional guarantees not to exceed one-half per cent (0.5%) of GDP in any financial year. The fiscal deficit target was to reduce to 3 per cent of GDP by 31 March 2021, and Government has committed to reach below 4.5 per cent by FY 2025-26.

test_cases.question: What is the debt sustainability indicator for FY 2023-24 and what does it indicate?

expected.answer_summary: The debt stabilization indicator for FY 2023-24 was positive at 4.09, which marks an improvement towards stability over FY 2022-23 (3.66). A positive indicator means the Debt-GDP ratio would eventually fall. The indicator is calculated as Quantum Spread plus Primary Deficit. For FY 2023-24, the quantum spread was 9.08 and primary deficit was ₹4.99 lakh crore. The interest spread (rate of GDP growth minus average interest cost) was 5.29 per cent. The YoY growth in GDP has outpaced YoY growth in Central Government Debt from FY 2021-22, indicating the economy has grown to be able to absorb and service the accumulated debt.
key_facts: Debt stabilization indicator for FY 2023-24 was 4.09 (positive)
Positive indicator means Debt-GDP ratio would eventually fall

Primary deficit was ₹4.99 lakh crore
Interest spread was 5.29 per cent
GDP growth outpaced debt growth from FY 2021-22

test_cases.question: What were the main audit recommendations for improving storage management?

expected.answer_summary: The audit made several recommendations: 1) GoI should aim at completing augmentation of storage facilities under National Policy on Handling, Storage and Transport of food grains. 2) GoI should rationalize statutory and non-statutory charges imposed by different State Governments to reduce acquisition cost. 3) Need to revisit existing Food Grains Stocking Norms considering Covid-19 situation. 4) FCI should make cost benefit analysis to augment own storage up to minimum stock level. 5) FCI should analyse causes of underutilisation of FSDs and construct modern storage facilities. 6) FCI should ensure compliance with FSSAI standards. 7) FCI should fix responsibility for losses/excess expenditure due to ineffective storage management.
key_facts: Complete storage facility augmentation under National Policy
Rationalize State Government charges
Revisit Food Grains Stocking Norms post-Covid
Cost benefit analysis for own storage augmentation
Ensure FSSAI compliance
Fix responsibility for losses
